edges.description: 11p15 ICR1 hypomethylation alters expression of IGF2 in the imprinted fetal-growth region.
Reduced IGF2 growth signaling impairs fetal and childhood growth.
Parent-of-origin imbalance is associated with reduced growth.

nodes.description: Loss of methylation at 11p15 ICR1 disrupts an imprinted growth-control region involving IGF2 and CDKN1C-associated pathways.

evidence.reference_title: 11p15 imprinting center region 1 loss of methylation is a common and specific cause of typical Russell-Silver syndrome: clinical scoring system and epigenetic-phenotypic correlations.
evidence.snippet: We identified loss of methylation (LOM) of 11p15 Imprinting Center Region 1 (ICR1) domain (including IGF-II) as a mechanism leading to RSS.

evidence.reference_title: Molecular and Clinical Opposite Findings in 11p15.5 Associated Imprinting Disorders: Characterization of Basic Mechanisms to Improve Clinical Management.
evidence.snippet: In fact, these changes directly or indirectly affect the expression of IGF2 and CDKN1C and their associated pathways, and thereby, cause growth disturbances as key features of both diseases.

meta.association: Rare causative gene and 11p15 imprinting target

evidence.reference_title: Silver-Russell syndrome secondary to rare (epi)genotypes exhibits phenotypic heterogeneity challenging clinical diagnosis.
evidence.snippet: Monogenic variants in imprinted (CDKN1C and IGF2) and non-imprinted (HMGA2 and PLAG1) genes are recognised as rare causes of SRS.

nodes.description: Feeding difficulty contributes to low BMI, failure to thrive, and early management complexity.

evidence.reference_title: Diagnosis and management of Silver-Russell syndrome: first international consensus statement.
evidence.snippet: Specific issues include growth failure, severe feeding difficulties, gastrointestinal problems, hypoglycaemia, body asymmetry, scoliosis, motor and speech delay and psychosocial challenges.

nodes.description: Recombinant human growth hormone therapy can improve height and other outcomes such as body composition, motor development, appetite, and hypoglycemia risk in SRS.

evidence.reference_title: Diagnosis and management of Silver-Russell syndrome: first international consensus statement.
evidence.snippet: The benefits of treating patients with SRS with growth hormone include improved body composition, motor development and appetite, reduced risk of hypoglycaemia and increased height.

nodes.description: Young children with SRS are vulnerable to hypoglycemia, particularly when intake is poor or fasting is prolonged.

evidence.reference_title: Diagnosis and management of Silver-Russell syndrome: first international consensus statement.
evidence.snippet: Specific issues include growth failure, severe feeding difficulties, gastrointestinal problems, hypoglycaemia, body asymmetry, scoliosis, motor and speech delay and psychosocial challenges.

meta.association: Rare causative gene and 11p15 imprinting target

evidence.reference_title: Molecular and Clinical Opposite Findings in 11p15.5 Associated Imprinting Disorders: Characterization of Basic Mechanisms to Improve Clinical Management.
evidence.snippet: In fact, these changes directly or indirectly affect the expression of IGF2 and CDKN1C and their associated pathways, and thereby, cause growth disturbances as key features of both diseases.

evidence.reference_title: Silver-Russell syndrome secondary to rare (epi)genotypes exhibits phenotypic heterogeneity challenging clinical diagnosis.
evidence.snippet: Monogenic variants in imprinted (CDKN1C and IGF2) and non-imprinted (HMGA2 and PLAG1) genes are recognised as rare causes of SRS.

nodes.description: Maternal UPD7 creates a parent-of-origin imbalance on chromosome 7 and is one of the two principal molecular causes of SRS.

evidence.reference_title: Executive functioning in adolescents and adults with Silver-Russell syndrome.
evidence.snippet: The two principal causes of SRS are loss of methylation on chromosome 11p15 (11p15 LOM) and maternal uniparental disomy of chromosome 7 (UPD(7)mat).

nodes.description: Management requires coordinated endocrine, nutrition, gastroenterology, genetics, developmental, orthopedic, and psychosocial care, with early focus on nutritional status while avoiding excessive rapid catch-up weight gain.

evidence.reference_title: Diagnosis and management of Silver-Russell syndrome: first international consensus statement.
evidence.snippet: The management of children with SRS requires an experienced, multidisciplinary approach.

evidence.reference_title: Diagnosis and management of Silver-Russell syndrome: first international consensus statement.
evidence.snippet: An early emphasis on adequate nutritional status is important, with awareness that rapid postnatal weight gain might lead to subsequent increased risk of metabolic disorders.

nodes.description: Growth restriction is the central downstream clinical phenotype, beginning before birth and continuing as postnatal growth failure and short stature.

evidence.reference_title: Diagnosis and management of Silver-Russell syndrome: first international consensus statement.
evidence.snippet: Specific issues include growth failure, severe feeding difficulties, gastrointestinal problems, hypoglycaemia, body asymmetry, scoliosis, motor and speech delay and psychosocial challenges.

nodes.description: The 11p15 ICR1 loss-of-methylation subtype reduces effective paternal IGF2 dosage, creating a molecular growth-signal deficit before overt prenatal and postnatal growth restriction.
